test: hot-standby mid-request fault tolerance (DEPLOY_READINESS §3 — turn the cold ~131s failover into seconds)
ring: N=4 scattered US 4090s (CA head · NV · IL · FL) + a HOT spare (WA) holding the victim's block
mechanism: vs the cold heal.py: (1) the spare is pre-launched WARM (weights already in VRAM, flex disk-cached) so it does NOT reload on failover — the reload was what dominated the cold 131s; (2) the victim's PREDECESSOR is REWIRED to the spare WITHOUT relaunching — the healer writes the spare endpoint to /root/.shard_next_<pred> and serve_spec_fast.mk_fwd re-reads it on its relink (engine change). No stage is relaunched, no weights reloaded.
interpretation: A middle node was killed mid-generation under load. The committed 423 tokens were preserved, the predecessor rewired to the WARM spare with NO model reload, the ring re-prefilled (prompt+423 committed) in 8.9s, and the request completed (447 tok, continuation byte-preserved, coherent output). End-to-end failover blip ~32.6s vs the cold heal.py's ~131s — the ~90s spare WEIGHT RELOAD is eliminated. Of the residual ~32s: detect 4.3s + re-prefill 8.9s + ~20s spent RE-LAUNCHING the coordinator process (a demo artifact — heal_hot.py spawns a fresh coordinator; a live deployment keeps the coordinator warm and re-drives in place, so the real recompute disruption is the 8.9s re-prefill).
honesty: Failover is now seconds-to-tens-of-seconds (32.6s, re-prefill 8.9s), not the cold ~131s. The headline win is removing the ~90s weight reload via the hot spare; the rewire is instant.

The ~20s coordinator-restart inside the 32.6s is incidental to this demo harness (fresh python coordinator each resume) — not a fundamental failover cost. With a warm coordinator the disruption ≈ the 8.9s re-prefill + ~4s detect.
Token preservation uses the SAME coordinate_pipe resume primitive proven in session 2's cold demo (189 tok preserved); only the spare-warmth + predecessor rewire are new here.

BLOCK-ONLY re-prefill (re-prefill just the dropped block via upstream activation checkpointing, vs the full prompt+committed) was NOT done — for a short prompt the full re-prefill is already 8.9s; it matters for long-context resume.
